steps.title: Determine what the opening modifier must describe
steps.content: The phrase “Far from being modern inventions” is an introductory modifier. It must directly precede—and describe—the grammatical subject of the main clause. The only things that could be called “modern inventions” in this sentence are drinking straws, so “drinking straws” must be the subject.

steps.title: Eliminate choices with the wrong subject and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘Sumerians in ancient Mesopotamia used drinking straws’: This places “Sumerians” as the subject. People are not “modern inventions,” so the modifier is misapplied. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘drinking straws were used by Sumerians in ancient Mesopotamia’: “Drinking straws” is the subject, correctly receiving the “modern inventions” modifier. ✓
  • Choice C — ‘the use of drinking straws by Sumerians in ancient Mesopotamia happened’: The subject is “the use,” an abstract noun phrase rather than drinking straws themselves; the modifier does not logically apply to “use.” ✗
  • Choice D — ‘ancient Mesopotamia was home to Sumerians who used drinking straws’: Places “ancient Mesopotamia” as the subject; a region is not a “modern invention.” ✗
That confirms B is correct.
